title: The Adventures Of An Elf.
content:
By Fedor Flinzer. Freely translated by J.H. Ewing.


I.

В В В В Dear children, listen whilst I tell
В В В В What to a certain Elf befell,
В В В В Who left his house and sallied forth
В В В В Adventure seeking, south and north,
В В В В And west and east, by path and field,
В В В В Resolved to conquer or to yield.
В В В В A thimble on his back he carried,
В В В В With a rose-twig his foes he parried.


II.

В В В В It was a sunny, bright, spring day,
В В В В When to the wood he took his way;
В В В В He knew that in a certain spot
В В В В A Bumble Bee his nest had got.
В В В В The Bee was out, the chance was good,
В В В В But just when grabbing all he could,
В В В В He heard the Bee behind him humming,
В В В В And only wished he'd heard him coming!


III.

В В В В In terror turned the tiny man,
В В В В And now a famous fight began:
В В В В The Bee flew round, and buzzed and stung,
В В В В The Elf his prickly rose-staff swung.
В В В В Now fiercely here, now wildly there,
В В В В He hit the Bee or fought the air.
В В В В At last one weighty blow descended:
В В В В The Bee was dead--the fight was ended.


IV.

В В В В Exhausted quite, he took a seat.
В В В В The honey tasted doubly sweet!
В В В В The thimble-full had been upset,
В В В В But still there were a few drops yet.
В В В В He licked his lips and blessed himself,
В В В В That he was such a lucky Elf,
В В В В And now might hope to live in clover;
В В В В But, ah! his troubles were not over!


V.

В В В В For at that instant, by his side,
В В В В A beast of fearful form he spied:
В В В В At first he thought it was a bear,
В В В В And headlong fell in dire despair.
В В В В He lost one slipper in the moss,
В В В В And this was not his only loss.
В В В В With paws and snout the beast was nimble,
В В В В And very soon cleared out the thimble.


VI.

В В В В This rifling of his honey-pot
В В В В Awoke our Elfin's wrath full hot.
В В В В He made a rope of linden bast,
В В В В By either end he held it fast,
В В В В And creeping up behind the beast,
В В В В Intent upon the honey feast,
В В В В Before it had the slightest inkling,
В В В В The rope was round it in a twinkling.


VII.

В В В В The mouse shrieked "Murder!" "Fire!" and "Thieves!"
В В В В And struggled through the twigs and leaves.
В В В В It pulled the reins with all its might,
В В В В Our hero only drew them tight.
В В В В Upon the mouse's back he leapt,
В В В В And like a man his seat he kept.
В В В В His steed was terribly affrighted,
В В В В But he himself was much delighted.


VIII.

В В В В "Gee up, my little horse!" he cried,
В В В В "I mean to have a glorious ride;
В В В В So bear me forth with lightning speed,
В В В В A Knight resolved on doughty deed.
В В В В The wide world we will gallop round,
В В В В And clear the hedges at one bound."
В В В В The mouse set off, the hero bantered,
В В В В And out into the world they cantered.


IX.

В В В В At last they rode up to an inn:
В В В В "Good Mr. Host, pray who's within?"
В В В В "My daughter serves the customers,
В В В В Before the fire the Tom-cat purrs."
В В В В For further news they did not wait--
В В В В The mouse sprang through the garden-gate--
В В В В They fled without a look behind them.
В В В В The question is--Did Thomas find them?
meaning:
This evocative piece by Juliana Horatia Ewing, titled "The Adventures Of An Elf.", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
